content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

June 2, 1982

Mr. Wayne Warner
P.O. Box 1302
Santa Maria, CA 93456

Re: No. 82-105

Dear Mr. Warner:

This letter is sent to confirm advice I gave you last
week on the telephone. This advice is provided pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114(b).1/

The basic facts are set forth in my letter of
January 28, 1982, to James Rozek, Counsel for the Santa
Maria Public Airport District. Since that letter was
written, my understanding is that the following events
occurred. The District's Board of Directors voted to
continue operation of the Village Mobilehome Park ("Park")
and has obtained an operating permit. The Board is
currently in the process of making managerial decisions
concerning the Park. The owners of the mobile homes in the
Park have formed an association. One proposal before the
Board is to recognize and accept the concept of the Board's
negotiating a lease with the association as the
representative of the Park's tenants.

You asked whether you would have a conflict of interest
in voting on this proposal.2/ I advised you that you did
not have a conflict of interest based on the following
analysis.

1/ All statutory references are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ You also mentioned to me that the Board was
evenly split, 2-2, on this issue. If you had a conflict of
interest, you could not vote just to break the tie vote.
Section 87102. In other words, the fact that there is a
deadlock on the Board without your vote is irrelevant to
this determination.

- 1 -

Mr. Wayne Warner
June 2, 1982
Page 2


     As I stated in my previous letter, the Political Reform
Act prohibits a public official from participating in or
attempting to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.  Section 87100.  A
public official has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on any investment or interest in
real property of the official worth $1,000 or more, on any
source of income to the official of $250 or more, or will
have a direct financial effect on the official himself or
herself.  Section 87103.

     In my letter to Mr. Rozek, I concluded that your
residency in the Park did not constitute an "interest in
real property" because the value did not exceed the $1,000
minimum threshold for a financial interest.  At that time,
the Board had made a decision to close the Park, and you
had neither the legal right or reasonable expectation to
remain in the Park for ten more months (the time it would
take you to pay $1,000 in rent).  Since you now can
reasonably expect to remain in the Park for at least ten
more months, the value of your leasehold exceeds $1,000.
However, it does not appear that the Board decision on the
proposal described above will have a material effect on
your leasehold interest.  A $1,000 effect on an interest in
real property is generally considered material.  See
Commission Regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18702(b)(2).  The decision at issue is a managerial policy
decision which will not foreseeably affect the amount of
rent to be paid or otherwise affect the value of your
leasehold.  Accordingly, you do not have to disqualify
yourself from participation in the decision on this ground.

     The only other possible ground for disqualification
would be based on an effect on your income.  As I explained
in my letter to Mr. Rozek, an official must disqualify
himself from a decision which will have a direct financial
effect on the official.  Under Commission guidelines, if an
official will incur or avoid expenses of $100 or more, it
is generally considered material.  See 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702(b)(3)(A)-(D).  In this situation, however, it
is not foreseeable that the decision before the Board will
directly cause you to avoid or incur out-of-pocket
payments.  Thus you would not be prohibited from
participating in the decision on this ground either.

Mr. Wayne Warner
June 2, 1982
Page 3


I trust that the foregoing discussion conforms to your
understanding of the advice I gave you. Please feel free
to contact me if you would like to discuss this letter or
if I can be of further assistance.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]
Diane Maura Fishburn
Counsel
Legal Division

DMF:km
Enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: Does a board member have a conflict of interest in voting on a proposal to negotiate a lease between the board and a mobile home owners' association when the board member is a resident of the mobile home park?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The board member does not have a disqualifying conflict of interest because while the leasehold interest exceeds the $1,000 threshold, the managerial policy decision at issue will not have a material financial effect on the rent or leasehold value, and no direct financial expenses are foreseeable.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR format document; letter is FPPC response confirming telephonic advice; references prior letter dated January 28, 1982 to James Rozek for basic facts; includes detailed statutory analysis of financial interest thresholds under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether you would have a conflict of interest in voting on this proposal.

CONCLUSION: Accordingly, you do not have to disqualify yourself from participation in the decision on this ground. The only other possible ground for disqualification would be based on an effect on your income. In this situation, however, it is not foreseeable that the decision before the Board will directly cause you to avoid or incur out-of-pocket payments. Thus you would not be prohibited from participating in the decision on this ground either.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel advises Mr. Wayne Warner, a resident board member of the Santa Maria Public Airport District's Village Mobilehome Park, that he does not have a conflict of interest under Government Code Section 87100 when voting on a proposal to negotiate a lease with a mobile home owners' association.
